one_line_takeaway: A single-author synthesis of nine adversarial-robustness-evaluation papers plus an open-source framework (AUTO-ART) that chains prior-work proxy metrics (RDI from Song et al., FOSC from Li et al.) into a 3-signal pre-screening gate, validated only on 10 CIFAR-10 RobustBench models where it reaches Kendall tau=0.82 rank agreement with full AutoAttack at ~30x lower compute and confirms a 23.5pp average-vs-worst-case multi-norm robustness gap.
key_finding: On the ten-model RobustBench CIFAR-10 l_inf validation set: RDI rankings agree with full AutoAttack rankings at Kendall tau = 0.82 (95% CI 0.71-0.93), with an OLS trend R^2=0.91 between RDI score and robust accuracy; RDI screening costs ~12 GPU-seconds per model versus ~360 GPU-seconds for full AutoAttack (~30x speedup), with 5-6x end-to-end pipeline improvement reported. The pre-screening gate (FOSC+RDI+WB/BB ensemble, tuned to a >=92% detection target) flagged 2 of the 10 models (Sehwag2021_ResNest, Wong2020Fast) as showing signs of unreliable/masked gradients, consistent with their lower RDI scores and robust accuracy. Multi-norm evaluation showed average multi-norm robustness dropping 12.3 pp (mean across the 10 models) relative to single-norm l_inf reporting, and worst-case (min-over-norms) robustness dropping 23.5 pp; the gap between multi-norm average and multi-norm worst-case was itself 11.2 pp -- i.e. even averaging across norms still overstates true worst-case robustness. The paper frames this as corroborating (not independently discovering) the average-vs-worst-case divergence finding of Dai et al. (corpus source C2, MultiRobustBench), applied here to the RobustBench l_inf leaderboard specifically.
core_contribution: Two linked contributions. (1) A structured literature synthesis of nine peer-reviewed/benchmark sources (ICML, NeurIPS, ICLR, UAI, 2020-2026) on adversarial robustness evaluation, applying seven analysis protocols (citation-chain tracing, gap scanning, methodology auditing, cross-paper synthesis, assumption stress-testing, knowledge mapping, lay distillation) to surface five direct contradictions in the field (e.g. 'fixed attack suites are reliable' vs 'new attacks invert leaderboard rankings') and five ranked research gaps. (2) AUTO-ART, an open-source Python framework that operationalizes those gaps: it integrates a pre-screening gate combining three signals -- FOSC gradient-masking diagnostics (originally from Li et al., corpus source C7), the Robustness Diagnostic Index RDI (originally from Song et al., corpus source C5), and a white-box/black-box discrepancy check -- ahead of expensive full attack runs, plus multi-norm (l1/l2/l_inf/semantic/spatial) worst-case evaluation, 50+ attacks across seven categories, 28 defence modules, adaptive/memory-guided attack selection, and compliance mapping to NIST AI RMF, OWASP LLM Top 10/Agentic Top 10, and EU AI Act Article 15. The paper's own novel technical work is the integration/traceability (explicit gap-to-module mapping, Table 6), the 3-signal ensemble gate, and its empirical validation -- not the invention of RDI or FOSC individually, which are adopted from prior corpus papers.
threat_model: Evaluator/defender-side threat model for assessing ML robustness claims, not an attacker-side exploit paper. Threats considered: (i) optimistic robustness estimates from single-norm (l_inf-only) evaluation that miss l1/l2/semantic/spatial perturbations; (ii) gradient masking/obfuscated gradients inflating attack-based robustness estimates in both deterministic and stochastic (diffusion-purification) defence pipelines; (iii) under-sampling of randomized-defence draws; (iv) premature generalization from stylized CTF-style benchmarks to production code and LLM/agentic settings. The framework's own empirical evaluation is restricted to CIFAR-10 image classifiers under l_inf/l2/l1 evasion attacks; LLM/agent/NLP threat coverage (jailbreaks, prompt injection, RAG poisoning) is present in the framework's attack catalogue and compliance mapping but is not exercised in the paper's reported experiments.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: corpus for the synthesis is only nine sources, so broader systematic reviews may reveal different gap rankings; AUTO-ART does not yet implement certified multi-norm training or longitudinal benchmark versioning; pre-screening gate accuracy depends on model architecture and FOSC is less informative for non-differentiable components; all empirical evaluation uses CIFAR-10 RobustBench models exclusively, with generalization to ImageNet-scale, multimodal, or production LLM/agent settings undemonstrated; runtime benchmarks are single-GPU only, distributed scaling untested. Threats-to-validity section separately notes: pre-screening thresholds (tau_mask=0.1, WB/BB gap 0.15) were tuned on this same CIFAR-10 leaderboard and may need recalibration elsewhere; Kendall tau measures ordinal rank agreement only, not calibration of RDI's absolute values for deployment decisions. The paper explicitly caveats its own novelty claim as resting on the specific combination of synthesis + gap-driven architecture, not on any individual component (attack ensembles, proxy metrics, compliance mapping) which it acknowledges have precedents in cited work.

quantitative_results: Kendall tau = 0.82 (95% CI 0.71-0.93) between RDI rankings and full AutoAttack rankings
OLS R^2 = 0.91 (n=10) for RDI score vs robust accuracy trend
~12 GPU-seconds per model for RDI screening vs ~360 GPU-seconds for full AutoAttack (~30x speedup); 5-6x end-to-end pipeline improvement
Pre-screening gate (FOSC+RDI+WB/BB) tuned to >=92% detection on 12 known-masking configurations; flagged 2/10 RobustBench models
Average multi-norm robustness drop: 12.3 pp (mean, 10 models) vs single l_inf
Worst-case multi-norm robustness drop: 23.5 pp (mean, 10 models) vs single l_inf
Gap between multi-norm average and multi-norm worst-case: 11.2 pp
Model robust-accuracy range in validation set: Wang2023Better_WRN-70 70.69% (highest) to Wong2020Fast 43.21% (lowest)
346+ automated tests passing across Python 3.9-3.12 in a five-phase CI/CD pipeline
baselines_compared: Full AutoAttack (Croce & Hein ensemble: APGD-CE, APGD-DLR, FAB, Square Attack) as ground-truth robustness reference
Single-norm (l_inf only) evaluation vs the paper's multi-norm (l1/l2/l_inf) average and worst-case evaluation
Single-signal ablations vs the 3-signal (FOSC + RDI + WB/BB discrepancy) ensemble gate
